key_points: Ratios and proportions are fundamental tools for solving comparison and distribution problems
Cross multiplication is the most reliable method to verify proportions
Representative fraction expresses the relationship between map distance and actual distance
Pie charts convert data proportions into angular sectors of a circle
Direct proportion maintains constant ratios between quantities
Inverse proportion maintains constant products between quantities
Many real-life situations such as work problems and speed-time relations follow proportional reasoning
examples: Map scale example: 4.5 cm on map represents 270 km
Pie chart example: 15% data corresponds to 54°
Work example: If workers double, time becomes half
exam_tips: Identify proportional relationships before applying formulas
Convert percentages into angles correctly in pie chart questions
Use ratio sums carefully when dividing quantities
Check units when solving map scale problems
In work problems, remember that workers and time are inversely proportional
